narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 36 and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 62.5% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on how your academic record stacks up against patterns seen in thousands of past students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is below the typical student's 5.5-ish average and signaled to the model a dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Nearly as important was your Year 1 decile of 2, again lower than the average around 5.7, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum in a competitive field like law. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was the top factor pulling in the opposite direction, toward predicting success, as it was close to the dataset average of 36.7 and showed strong reasoning ability compared to many peers. These three elements—your Year 3 and Year 1 deciles outweighing the LSAT—dominated the model's decision, outweighing other details like your GPA or family income. For students like you, who share similar demographic backgrounds, the model has learned that early and mid-undergrad deciles are especially predictive because they reflect sustained grit in rigorous settings, unlike one-off test scores. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of applicants, it didn't sway things much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes these bar-pass patterns to minimize risks for the program. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA-improvement path, and reapply with that stronger academic trajectory. Schools value persistence, and addressing these key areas could flip the prediction in your favor next time.
